---
title: "Git & Version Control"
subtitle: "Track changes, collaborate with others, and never lose your work again."
author: "Curt Rode"
date: last-modified
difficulty: intermediate
time-estimate: "50 min"
format:
  html:
    code-tools: false
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

Have you ever had multiple versions of a file like `final_paper.docx`, `final_paper_v2.docx`, `final_paper_FINAL.docx`, and `final_paper_FINAL_really.docx`? Version control solves these problems elegantly, and Git is the tool that makes it possible.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## Learning Objectives
By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

- Explain why version control matters for DH work
- Set up Git and configure it for your projects
- Use the core Git workflow (add → commit → push)
- Collaborate using GitHub
- Recover from common mistakes
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## What You Need
- Familiarity with the command line
- Git installed (we’ll cover setup)
- A free GitHub account
:::

## Why Version Control Matters

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="version-control-why">
  <h3>Quick Check: Why Use Version Control?</h3>
  <p>Your Python script worked yesterday, but today it's broken and you don't know what changed. What's the best solution?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="vc-quiz-1" value="backup">
    Hope you saved a backup copy somewhere
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="vc-quiz-1" value="git">
    Use Git to see exactly what changed and restore the working version
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="vc-quiz-1" value="memory">
    Try to remember what you changed and undo it manually
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Exactly!</strong> Git tracks every change so you can restore a working version instantly.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p>Git lets you see the exact change history and roll back safely—no guessing required.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Installing and Configuring Git

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Mac

**Option 1: Xcode Command Line Tools (Recommended)**  
Run:
```
git --version
```
If Git isn’t installed, macOS will prompt you to install Xcode Command Line Tools.

**Option 2: Homebrew**
```
brew install git
```

## PC

1. Download Git for Windows: <https://git-scm.com/download/win>  
2. Install with recommended defaults  
3. Verify in PowerShell or Git Bash:
```
git --version
```
:::

### Configure Your Identity

```
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@university.edu"
git config --list
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Use the same email as your GitHub account so your contributions are linked properly.
:::

## Alternative: GitHub Desktop

If the command line feels intimidating, **GitHub Desktop** provides a friendly GUI. It’s great for learning the concepts before switching to CLI for speed and automation.

## The Git Mental Model

Files move through three states:

1. **Working directory** (your files)
2. **Staging area** (`git add`)
3. **Repository** (`git commit`)

## Your First Repository

```
mkdir victorian-novels-analysis
cd victorian-novels-analysis
git init
mkdir data scripts results
echo "# Victorian Novels Analysis" > README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Initial commit: Add project README"
```

## The Core Git Workflow

```
git status
git add .
git commit -m "Describe your change"
git push
```

### Common Commands

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `git status` | Show state |
| `git add` | Stage changes |
| `git commit` | Save snapshot |
| `git log` | View history |
| `git diff` | See changes |
| `git restore` | Undo local changes |

## Writing Good Commit Messages

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="commit-messages">
  <h3>Quick Check: Commit Messages</h3>
  <p>You fixed a crash when processing files with accented characters. Which message is best?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="commit-quiz" value="bad1">
    <code>"bug fix"</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="commit-quiz" value="bad2">
    <code>"fixed the thing"</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="commit-quiz" value="good">
    <code>"Fix crash when processing files with accented characters"</code>
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Yes.</strong> It describes what changed and why it matters.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p>Good messages explain both <strong>what</strong> and <strong>why</strong>.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Working with GitHub

### Create a GitHub Account
1. Go to <https://github.com>
2. Sign up with your university email
3. Verify your email address

### Connect Local Repo to GitHub

```
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/project.git
git push -u origin main
```

## Branching: Safe Experimentation

```
git switch -c experiment
# make changes...
git switch main
git merge experiment
git branch -d experiment
```

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="branching">
  <h3>Quick Check: Safe Experimentation</h3>
  <p>What's the safest way to try a risky new analysis approach?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="branch-quiz" value="copy">
    Copy the whole project to a new folder
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="branch-quiz" value="branch">
    Create a Git branch to isolate your experiment
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="branch-quiz" value="careful">
    Edit carefully and hope nothing breaks
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Correct!</strong> Branches let you experiment without risking your main work.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p>Branches are the safest and fastest way to test new ideas.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## The .gitignore File

Create a `.gitignore` file to avoid committing:
- API keys / secrets  
- Large datasets  
- System files (`.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`)  
- Virtual environments  

Example:
```
__pycache__/
venv/
.env
.DS_Store
data/raw/*.csv
```

## Recovering from Mistakes

```
git restore filename.py
git restore --staged filename.py
git log --oneline
git show <commit>:filename.py
```

## Quick Reference Card

::: {.callout-tip}
## Git Cheat Sheet
- `git status` — check state  
- `git add .` — stage all changes  
- `git commit -m "msg"` — save  
- `git push` / `git pull` — sync  
- `git branch` / `git switch -c name` — branching  
:::

## Next Steps

You’re ready for **Regular Expressions**, where you’ll learn to find patterns in text quickly and precisely.
